debug_info.goal:
n : ℕ
δL : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) → ℝ
hδL0 : δL 0 = 0
h : ClassicalMechanics.Lagrangian.IsTotalTimeDerivative n fun x v x_1 => δL v
⊢ ∃ g, ∀ (v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)), δL v = inner ℝ g v

failed_attempts:
obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick any r₀ and t₀, extract the linear map from the derivative
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  have hderiv := hF_diff.differentiableAt (x := (r₀, t₀))
  -- Extract g from the first component of the derivative
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- Define g as the vector such that L(v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  have hL : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  -- Since δL 0 = 0, we have L(0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by rw [← hL]; exact hδL0
  -- L is linear, so L(v, 1) = L(v, 0) + L(0, 1) = L(v, 0)
  have hsplit : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  have hsimp : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) := fun v => by rw [hsplit, h0, add_zero]
  -- L(·, 0) is a continuous linear map on EuclideanSpace, hence representable via inner product
  let L' : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →ₗ[ℝ] ℝ := {
    toFun := fun v => L (v, 0)
    map_add' := fun x y => by simp [L.map_add]
    map_smul' := fun c x => by simp [L.map_smul]
  }
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g with L'(v) = ⟪g, v⟫
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) |>.surjective
    ⟨L', L.cont.comp (continuous_prod_mk.mpr ⟨continuous_id, continuous_const⟩) |>.clm_comp⟩
  use g
  intro v
  rw [hL, hsimp]
  have : (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) g) v = ⟪g, v⟫_ℝ :=
    InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply ℝ g v
  rw [← this, hg]
  rfl
sorry

obtain ⟨F, hFdiff, hF⟩ := h
  -- At (0, 0), δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1)
  -- Since δL 0 = 0, we have fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1) = 0
  -- So the time component of the derivative is 0
  -- Thus δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0)
  -- This is a continuous linear map in v, so it equals ⟪g, v⟫ for some g
  have h0 : fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1) = 0 := by
    rw [← hF 0 0 0, hδL0]
  use (fun i => fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (EuclideanSpace.single i 1, 0))
  intro v
  rw [hF 0 v 0]
  have hlin : fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0) + fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1) := by
    rw [← (fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)).map_add]
    congr 1
    simp only [Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add]
  rw [hlin, h0, add_zero]
  rw [EuclideanSpace.inner_piLp_equiv_symm, Finset.inner_apply]
  conv_lhs => rw [← EuclideanSpace.sum_single v]
  simp only [Prod.mk_zero_zero, map_sum, ContinuousLinearMap.map_smul]
  congr 1
  ext i
  rw [inner_smul_right, mul_comm]
  congr 1
  simp only [EuclideanSpace.single, PiLp.equiv_symm_single]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- At any fixed point (r₀, t₀), the derivative is a continuous linear map
  -- Since δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) for all r, t, and the LHS doesn't depend on r, t
  -- the linear part in v must be constant
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) restricted to the velocity component gives us g
  have hlin : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  -- Extract the linear functional on the velocity space
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- Define g as the Riesz representative of v ↦ L (v, 0)
  have hL : IsBoundedLinearMap ℝ (fun v => L (v, 0)) := by
    exact IsBoundedLinearMap.comp L.isBoundedLinearMap (IsBoundedLinearMap.prod_left 0)
  -- Use Riesz representation
  let g := (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm 
           ⟨fun v => L (v, 0), hL.toContinuousLinearMap⟩
  use g
  intro v
  -- We have δL v = L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1)
  have h1 : L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  -- And L (0, 1) = δL 0 = 0
  have h2 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by
    have : L (0, 1) = δL 0 := (hlin 0).symm
    rw [this, hδL0]
  rw [hlin v, h1, h2, add_zero]
  -- Now L (v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  simp only [g, InnerProductSpace.toDual_symm_apply]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hFdiff, hF⟩ := h
  -- At any fixed (r₀, t₀), the derivative gives us δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1)
  -- Since this is independent of r₀, t₀, we can pick any point
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The fderiv is a continuous linear map, so (v, 1) ↦ fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) is linear in v
  -- plus a constant (the contribution from the 1 component)
  have key : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF r₀ v t₀
  -- Using hδL0: δL 0 = 0, we have fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0 := by rw [← hδL0, ← key]
  -- So δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) - fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0)
  have hlin : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) := by
    intro v
    rw [key v, ← h0]
    have := (fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)).map_add (v, 0) (0, 1)
    simp only [Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add] at this
    linarith [this]
  -- The map v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) is a continuous linear functional
  -- By Riesz representation, it equals ⟪g, v⟫ for some g
  let L : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := (fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm L
  intro v
  rw [hlin]
  simp only [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply, InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hFdiff, hF⟩ := h
  -- The key observation: δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) for all r, t
  -- Since δL doesn't depend on r or t, the fderiv must be constant in (r,t)
  -- At (0, 0), fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) is a continuous linear map
  -- Since δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1), and δL 0 = 0, we have fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1) = 0
  -- The map v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0) is linear, giving us our g
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)
  have hL : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 1) := fun v => hF 0 v 0
  have hL0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by simp only [← hL, hδL0]
  -- L is a continuous linear map, so L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = L (v, 0)
  have hLadd : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  have hLv : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) := fun v => by rw [hL, hLadd, hL0, add_zero]
  -- The map v ↦ L (v, 0) is linear, so by Riesz representation, there exists g
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  have hφ : ∀ v, δL v = φ v := fun v => hLv v
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDual_surjective (E := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) φ
  use g
  intro v
  rw [hφ, ← hg]
  simp [InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
sorry

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- The derivative fderiv ℝ F (r, t) is a continuous linear map
  -- Since δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) for all r, t, the map v ↦ δL v is linear
  -- We extract g from this linear structure
  have h_indep : ∀ r₁ r₂ t₁ t₂ v, fderiv ℝ F (r₁, t₁) (v, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (r₂, t₂) (v, 1) := by
    intros r₁ r₂ t₁ t₂ v
    rw [← hF_eq r₁ v t₁, hF_eq r₂ v t₂]
  -- Pick a reference point (0, 0)
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)
  have hL : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq 0 v 0
  -- L is a continuous linear map, so L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1)
  have hL_add : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  -- δL 0 = 0 implies L (0, 1) = 0
  have hL01 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by
    have : δL 0 = L (0, 1) := by rw [hL 0]; simp [L.map_add]
    rw [← this, hδL0]
  -- So δL v = L (v, 0), which is linear in v
  have hδL_linear : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) := fun v => by rw [hL, hL_add, hL01, add_zero]
  -- The map v ↦ L (v, 0) is a continuous linear functional on EuclideanSpace
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that L (v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm φ
  intro v
  rw [hδL_linear]
  simp only [φ, ContinuousLinearMap.comp_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.inl_apply]
  rw [InnerProductSpace.toDual_symm_apply]

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick any fixed r₀ and t₀
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) restricted to the v-component gives our g
  have hlin : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  -- From hδL0, we get fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0 := by rw [← hlin 0, hδL0]
  -- The fderiv is linear, so fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) = fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) + fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1)
  have hlin_split : ∀ v, fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) + fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) := by
    intro v
    have := (fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)).map_add (v, 0) (0, 1)
    simp only [Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add] at this
    exact this
  -- So δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0)
  have hδL_eq : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) := by
    intro v
    rw [hlin v, hlin_split v, h0, add_zero]
  -- The map v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) is a continuous linear map on EuclideanSpace
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that this equals ⟪g, v⟫
  let L : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := (fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm L
  intro v
  rw [hδL_eq]
  simp only [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply, InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
  rfl

  -- Pick any point (r₀, t₀) to extract the linear functional
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  have hderiv : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hδL r₀ v t₀
  -- From δL 0 = 0, we get fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0 := by rw [← hderiv 0, hδL0]
  -- Define g using the linear map restricted to the velocity component
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- L is a continuous linear map, L (v, 1) = δL v
  -- L (0, 1) = 0 means the time component contributes 0
  -- So L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = L (v, 0)
  -- L (v, 0) is linear in v, so by Riesz representation, L (v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  have hlin : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have := L.map_add (v, 0) (0, 1)

  -- L restricted to first component is continuous linear

  -- By Riesz representation
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm L'
  intro v
  rw [hsimp]
  simp only [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply, InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick any point (r₀, t₀) and extract the gradient with respect to position
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The fderiv at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map from (E × ℝ) to ℝ
  -- Since δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) for all r, t, and the LHS is independent of r, t,
  -- the map v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 0) must be constant across all (r, t)
  -- Let g be the Riesz representation of v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0)
  have h1 : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  have h0 : fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) = 0 := by
    rw [← h1, hδL0]
  -- fderiv is linear, so fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) = fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) + fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1)
  have hlin : ∀ v, fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) + fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) := by
    intro v
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, ContinuousLinearMap.map_add]
  -- So δL v = fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0)
  have hδL_eq : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) := by
    intro v
    rw [h1, hlin, h0, add_zero]
  -- The map v ↦ fderiv F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) is linear, representable by inner product
  let L : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := (fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  have hL : ∀ v, L v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) := fun v => rfl
  -- Use Riesz representation
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm L
  intro v
  rw [hδL_eq, ← hL]
  simp only [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply, InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hF, hδL⟩ := h
  -- At r=0, t=0, the derivative fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) is a continuous linear map
  -- δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1) for all v
  -- Since δL 0 = 0, we have fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1) = 0
  -- So δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0) which is linear in v
  have h0 : ∀ r t, fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (0, 1) = 0 := fun r t => by
    simpa using hδL r 0 t ▸ hδL0
  -- The map v ↦ δL v equals v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1)
  -- = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0) + fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1)
  -- = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0) (since the second term is 0)
  -- This is a continuous linear map in v, hence an inner product
  have hlin : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 0) := fun v => by
    have := hδL 0 v 0
    simp only at this
    rw [this]
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, (0 : ℝ)) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, (fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)).map_add, h0 0 0, add_zero]
  -- Now fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) restricted to (v, 0) is a continuous linear functional on v
  let L : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := (fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ _)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm L
  intro v
  rw [hlin]
  simp only [L, ContinuousLinearMap.comp_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.inl_apply]
  rw [InnerProductSpace.toDual_symm_apply]

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- The key mathematical insight: since δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) for all r, t,
  -- and δL doesn't depend on r or t, the derivative must have a special form.
  -- In particular, fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 0) must be independent of r and t.
  -- This forces ∇_r F to be constant, giving us our g.
  sorry

obtain ⟨F, hF, hδL⟩ := h
  have h0 : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1) := fun v => hδL 0 v 0
  have hF0 : fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (0, 1) = 0 := by rw [← h0, hδL0]
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)
  have hLadd : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  have hL : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) := fun v => by simp [hLadd, hF0]
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ _)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm φ
  intro v
  rw [h0, hL, InnerProductSpace.toDual_symm_apply]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- The derivative of F at any point (r, t) applied to (v, 1) equals δL v
  -- Since this is independent of r, t, the partial derivative w.r.t. position must be constant
  -- Choose g to be this constant gradient
  use fun i => fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (EuclideanSpace.single i 1, 0)
  intro v
  -- The key is that fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 0) + fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (0, 1)
  -- and since δL v doesn't depend on r, t, the spatial part must be linear in v
  sorry

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- The derivative at any point (r, t) applied to (v, 1) equals δL v
  -- Since this is independent of r and t, we can evaluate at (0, 0)
  have key : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (0, 0) (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq 0 v 0
  -- The fderiv is a continuous linear map, so we can decompose it
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)
  -- L : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) × ℝ →L[ℝ] ℝ
  -- L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1)
  have hL_add : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  -- δL 0 = 0 gives us L (0, 1) = 0 - L (0, 0) = 0
  have hL01 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by
    have := key 0
    simp [hδL0] at this
    convert this using 1
    simp [L]
  -- So δL v = L (v, 0)
  have hδL_eq : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) := fun v => by
    rw [key v, ← hL_add, hL01, add_zero]
  -- L (v, 0) is linear in v, so there exists g with L (v, 0) = ⟨g, v⟩
  -- Use Riesz representation
  let L' : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  have hL'_eq : ∀ v, L' v = L (v, 0) := fun v => rfl
  -- By finite-dimensional Riesz, L' v = ⟨g, v⟩ for some g
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm ⟨L', L'.cont⟩
  intro v
  rw [hδL_eq]
  simp only [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply, InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
  rfl
exact sorry

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick arbitrary r₀ and t₀
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) gives us the linear functional
  have h1 : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  -- The fderiv is a continuous linear map, so fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) is linear in v
  -- plus a constant (from the 1 component)
  have hconst : δL 0 = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) := h1 0
  rw [hδL0] at hconst
  -- Define g as the partial derivative with respect to the first component
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- We need to extract the part that acts on v
  -- Since L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) by linearity
  have hlin : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have := L.map_add (v, 0) (0, 1)
    simp at this
    exact this
  -- So δL v = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = L (v, 0) + 0 = L (v, 0)
  have h2 : L (0, 1) = 0 := hconst.symm
  have h3 : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) := fun v => by
    rw [h1 v, hlin v, h2, add_zero]
  -- L (·, 0) is a continuous linear map on EuclideanSpace, hence representable by inner product
  let L' : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ _)
  have hL' : ∀ v, L' v = L (v, 0) := fun v => rfl
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that L' v = ⟪g, v⟫
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDualEquiv.surjective L'
  use g
  intro v
  rw [h3 v, ← hL' v, ← hg]
  simp [InnerProductSpace.toDualEquiv]
sorry
sorry

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Fix any r₀, t₀ and extract the linear map
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The fderiv at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  have hder := hF_diff.differentiableAt.hasFDerivAt
  -- Extract the component corresponding to the r-direction
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- Since δL v = L (v, 1) for all v, and δL 0 = 0, we have L (0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by simpa using hF_eq r₀ 0 t₀ ▸ hδL0
  -- L is linear, so L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = L (v, 0)
  have hLv : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) := fun v => by
    have := L.map_add (v, 0) (0, 1)
    simp only [Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add] at this
    rw [this, h0, add_zero]
  -- The map v ↦ L (v, 0) is a continuous linear functional on EuclideanSpace
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that L (v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDual_surjective (E := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) φ
  use g
  intro v
  have key : δL v = L (v, 1) := by
    have := hF_eq r₀ v t₀
    convert this
  rw [key, hLv]
  have : φ v = L (v, 0) := rfl
  rw [← this]
  rw [← hg]
  simp [InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]

obtain ⟨F, hF, hδL⟩ := h
  -- Pick arbitrary r₀ and t₀
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The fderiv at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- For any v, δL v = L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1)
  -- Since δL 0 = 0, we have L (0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by
    have := hδL r₀ 0 t₀
    simp only at this
    rw [hδL0] at this
    exact this.symm
  -- Define the linear map on the velocity component
  let Lv : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that Lv v = ⟪g, v⟫
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDualMap_surjective ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) Lv
  use g
  intro v
  have hLv : ∀ v, L (v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫_ℝ := fun v => by
    have : Lv v = ⟪g, v⟫_ℝ := by
      rw [← hg]
      simp [InnerProductSpace.toDualMap_apply]
    simpa [Lv] using this
  -- Now show δL v = L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = ⟪g, v⟫ + 0
  have key : ∀ r t, δL v = L (v, 1) := fun r t => by
    rw [hδL r v t, hδL r₀ v t₀]
  rw [key r₀ t₀]
  have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
  rw [this, L.map_add, h0, add_zero, hLv]

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick any r₀ and t₀, the derivative at that point gives us a continuous linear map
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The fderiv at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  have hderiv := hF_diff.differentiableAt.hasFDerivAt
  -- For any v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1)
  -- Since this holds for all r, t, the v-component of the derivative is constant
  -- Extract the linear functional on v
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- L is a continuous linear map from (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) × ℝ to ℝ
  -- We need the restriction to the first component
  let g := (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm 
           (ContinuousLinearMap.comp L (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ))
  use g
  intro v
  have h1 : δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  have h2 : δL 0 = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) := hF_eq r₀ 0 t₀
  rw [hδL0] at h2
  -- fderiv is linear, so fderiv F (r₀,t₀) (v,1) = fderiv F (r₀,t₀) (v,0) + fderiv F (r₀,t₀) (0,1)
  have hlin : fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 0) + fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (0, 1) := by
    rw [← (fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)).map_add]
    congr 1
    simp only [Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add]
  rw [h1, hlin, ← h2, add_zero]
  simp only [InnerProductSpace.toDual_symm_apply]
  rfl

obtain ⟨F, hFdiff, hF⟩ := h
  -- The derivative at any point (r, t) gives δL(v) = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1)
  -- Since this is independent of r and t, pick any point, say (0, 0)
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (0, 0)
  -- L is a continuous linear map from (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) × ℝ to ℝ
  -- Define g via the restriction to the first component
  let g : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ _).symm 
    (L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) ℝ))
  use g
  intro v
  have h1 : δL v = L (v, 1) := hF 0 v 0
  have h0 : δL 0 = L (0, 1) := hF 0 0 0
  rw [hδL0] at h0
  -- L(v, 1) = L(v, 0) + L(0, 1) = L(v, 0) + 0 = L(v, 0)
  have hlin : L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := by
    rw [← L.map_add]; congr; ext <;> simp
  rw [h0, add_zero] at hlin
  rw [h1, hlin]
  -- Now L(v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  simp only [ContinuousLinearMap.comp_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.inl_apply]
  rw [InnerProductSpace.toDual_symm_apply]
  rfl
sorry

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick any fixed r₀ and t₀
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) gives us a continuous linear map
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- Since δL v = L (v, 1) for all v, and δL 0 = 0, we have L (0, 1) = 0
  have hL0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by simp only [← hF_eq r₀ 0 t₀, hδL0]
  -- L is linear, so L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = L (v, 0)
  have hLv : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) := fun v => by
    have : L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := by
      rw [← L.map_add]; congr; ext <;> simp
    rw [this, hL0, add_zero]
  -- L (v, 0) is linear in v, so it's an inner product
  -- Define g via Riesz representation
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  -- By Riesz, there exists g such that φ v = ⟪g, v⟫
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDual_surjective ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) φ
  use g
  intro v
  rw [hF_eq r₀ v t₀, hLv]
  simp only [φ] at hg
  have : L (v, 0) = φ v := by simp [φ, ContinuousLinearMap.comp_apply]
  rw [this]
  rw [← hg]
  simp [InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]

obtain ⟨F, hF_diff, hF_eq⟩ := h
  -- Pick any fixed point (r₀, t₀) to extract the linear form
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) gives us a continuous linear map
  have hF_diff_at : DifferentiableAt ℝ F (r₀, t₀) := hF_diff.differentiableAt
  -- Extract the linear functional on the velocity component
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- Define g using the Riesz representation
  have : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 1) := fun v => hF_eq r₀ v t₀
  have h0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by simp only [← this, hδL0]
  -- Since δL v = L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) = L (v, 0) + 0 = L (v, 0)
  have hL_split : ∀ v, L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, L.map_add]
  have hL_v : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) := fun v => by
    rw [this v, hL_split v, h0, add_zero]
  -- The map v ↦ L (v, 0) is linear and continuous
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  have hφ : ∀ v, δL v = φ v := fun v => hL_v v
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that φ v = ⟪g, v⟫
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := InnerProductSpace.toDual_surjective (F := ℝ) (E := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) φ
  use g
  intro v
  rw [hφ v, ← hg]
  simp [InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]

obtain ⟨F, hFdiff, hF⟩ := h
  -- Pick any fixed r₀ and t₀
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The fderiv at (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  have hFderiv : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF r₀ v t₀
  -- Extract the linear part in v
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- L is a continuous linear map from (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) × ℝ) to ℝ
  -- We have δL v = L (v, 1) = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1)
  have hL : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) + L (0, 1) := fun v => by
    rw [hFderiv v]
    have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
    rw [this, (hFdiff.differentiableAt).hasFDerivAt.fderiv.map_add]
  -- From hδL0, we get L (0, 0) + L (0, 1) = 0, so L (0, 1) = 0
  have hL01 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by
    have h0 := hL 0
    simp only [hδL0] at h0
    simp only [Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add] at h0
    have : L (0, 0) = 0 := by simp [L, ContinuousLinearMap.map_zero]
    linarith
  -- So δL v = L (v, 0)
  have hδL_linear : ∀ v, δL v = L (v, 0) := fun v => by rw [hL, hL01, add_zero]
  -- L restricted to the first component is a continuous linear map on EuclideanSpace
  -- By Riesz representation, there exists g such that L (v, 0) = ⟪g, v⟫
  let L₁ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ _)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm L₁
  intro v
  rw [hδL_linear]
  simp only [L₁, ContinuousLinearMap.comp_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.inl_apply]
  rw [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
  simp [InnerProductSpace.toDual_apply]
sorry

obtain ⟨F, hFdiff, hF⟩ := h
  -- Pick arbitrary r₀, t₀ to get the derivative
  let r₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) := 0
  let t₀ : ℝ := 0
  -- The derivative at (r₀, t₀) applied to (v, 1) equals δL v
  -- Since δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r, t) (v, 1) for all r, t, in particular at (r₀, t₀)
  -- Define g using the representation of continuous linear maps on Euclidean space
  have hlin : ∀ v, δL v = fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) := fun v => hF r₀ v t₀
  -- The map v ↦ fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) (v, 1) is linear in v
  -- Using that fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀) is a continuous linear map
  let L := fderiv ℝ F (r₀, t₀)
  -- Define g as the Riesz representative of the functional v ↦ L (v, 0)
  let φ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] ℝ := L.comp (ContinuousLinearMap.inl ℝ _ ℝ)
  use (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))).symm φ
  intro v
  rw [hlin v]
  simp only [LinearIsometryEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
  have : (v, (1 : ℝ)) = (v, 0) + (0, 1) := by simp
  rw [this, L.map_add]
  -- L (0, 1) is a constant, and δL 0 = 0 means L (0, 1) = 0
  have h0 : L (0, 1) = 0 := by
    have := hlin 0
    simp only [hδL0] at this
    convert this.symm
    simp
  rw [h0, add_zero]
  rfl
